Then a random vector $g$ is a stochastic gradient for $f$ at the point $x$ if $\mathbb{E}[g] \in \partial f(x)$, or \[f(y) \geq f(x) + \langle \mathbb{E}[g], y-x \rangle\] To say a bit more formally, we define stochastic gradient oracle: Definition A stochastic gradient oracle for the function $f$ consists of a triple $(g, \mathcal{S}, P)$, where $\mathcal{S}$ is the sample space, $P$ is the probability distribution, and $g: \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$ is a mapping that satisfies \[\mathbb{E}_P [ g(x,S) ] = \int_{\mathcal{S}} g(x,s) dP(s) \in \partial f(x)\] where $S\in \mathcal{S}$ is a sample drawn from $P$. Often, with some abuse of notation, we will use $g$ or $g(x)$ for shorthand of the random vector $g(x,S)$. Stochastic Programming Definition Consider a problem \[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ f(x):= \mathbb{E}_P [ F(x; S)]\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ x\in C \end{aligned}\] where $S\in \mathcal{S}$ is a random variable with probability $P$. For each $s\in \mathcal{S}$, the function $F(x; s) $ is convex. If we let \[g(x,s) \in \partial_x F(x;s),\] then $g(x,S)$ is a stochastic gradient of $f$ when we draw $S\sim P$. In other words, \[\mathbb{E}_P [g(x,s) ] \in \partial f(x)\] proof) \[\begin{aligned} f(y) = \mathbb{E}_P [F(y;S)]& \geq \mathbb{E}_P [F(x;S) + \langle g(x,S), y-x\rangle ] \\& = \mathbb{E}_P [F(x;S)] + \langle \mathbb{E}_P [g(x,S)], y-x \rangle \\ & = f(x) + \langle \mathbb{E}_P [g(x,S)], y-x \rangle \end{aligned}\] Example 1 Consider the objective function \[f(x) = \frac{1}{m}\|Ax- b\|_1 = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m |\langle a_i, x \rangle -b_i | = \mathbb{E}_{uni}\Big [ | \langle a_i, x\rangle - b_i | \Big ]\] where $a_i, b_i$ can be construed as a random variable. Thus, if we define the \[g(x,(a_i, b_i) ) = a_i\text{sign}(\langle a_i,x\rangle -b_i) \in \partial _x |\langle a_i, x \rangle - b_i |\] we can see that $g$ is the stochastic subgradient of $x$, successfully satisfying the condition \[\mathbb{E}_P [g(x,(a_i, b_i)] = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^M a_i \text{sign} (\langle a_i, x \rangle- b_i ) \in \partial f(x)\] Example 2 Given any problem in which one has a large dataset \(\{s_1, \cdots, s_m \}\) and we wish to minimize the sum \[f(x) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m F(x; s_i ),\] then drawing an index \(i \in \{1, \cdots, m\}\) unifotmly at random and using $g\in \partial_x F(x; s_i)$ is a stochastic gradient. More generally, the expectation the expectation $\mathbb{E}_P [F(x;S)]= f(x)$ might be intractable to compute when $S$ is a high-dimensional distribution. In these cases, we might just use stochastic subgradient rather than full gradient. Stochastic subgradient Method Algorithm compute a stochastic subgradient $g_k$ at the point $x_k$, where \[\mathbb{E} [g_k \ \vert \ x_k] \in \partial f(x_k)\] Perform the projected subgradient step \(x_{k+1} := \pi_C(x_k - \alpha_k g_k).\) Example \[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ f(x)= \frac{1}{m} \|Ax - b\|_1 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m | \langle a_i, x\rangle - b_i |\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \|x_2 \|\leq R. \end{aligned}\] Using a stochastic subgradient \[g= a_i \text{sign} (\langle a_i, x\rangle-b_i)\] with a fixed stepsize, we have Covergence Properties Assumption 1) Feasible set $C$ is compact, and there exists some $R<\infty$ such that \(\|x^\star - x\|_2\leq R\) for all $x\in C$. 2) For all $x\in C$, we have bounded stochastic subgradient \(\mathbb{E}[\|g(x,S)\|_2^2]\leq M^2\). Theorem Let \(\{x_k\}\) be a non-increasing sequence of stepsizes, and let \(\bar{x}_K = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K x_k\). Then, \[\mathbb{E}[f(\bar{x}_k) - f(x^\star) ] \leq \frac{R^2}{2K\alpha_K} + \frac{1}{2K} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k M^2\] Proof Let $f’(x)$ be the valid (full) subgradient of $f$, and $g(x,S)$ be its stochastic subgradient. \(f'(x) := \mathbb{E} [g(x,S)] \in \partial f(x)\) Then, define the error of subgradient: \[\xi _k := g_k - f'(x_k)\] Then, by the non-expansive property of $\pi_c$, we have \[\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2} \|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 & = \frac{1}{2} \|\pi_c (x_k - \alpha_k g_k) - \pi_c (x^\star) \|_2^2\\ & \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star \|_2^2 \\ & = \frac{1}{2}\|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \alpha_k \langle g_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2\\ & = \frac{1}{2}\|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \alpha_k \langle g_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle + \alpha_k \langle f'(x_k), x_k - x^\star \rangle + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2 - \alpha_k \langle f'(x_k), x_k - x^\star \rangle\\ & = \frac{1}{2}\|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \alpha_k \langle \xi _k , x_k - x^\star \rangle + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2 - \alpha_k \langle f'(x_k), x_k - x^\star \rangle\\ & \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \alpha_k \langle \xi_k , x_k - x^\star \rangle + \frac{1}{2} \alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2 - \alpha_k [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ] \end{aligned}\] Rearranging the inequality above, we have \[[f(x_k) - f(x^\star)] \leq \frac{1}{2\alpha_k} \bigg [ \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \|x_{k+1}- x^\star \|_2^2 \bigg ] +\frac{\alpha_k}{2}\|g_k\|_2^2 - \langle \xi_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle\] Summing this inequality, we have \[\begin{aligned} \sum_{k=1}^K [f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ] \leq \frac{R^2}{2\alpha_K} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \|g_k \|_2^2 - \sum_{k=1}^K \langle \xi_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle \end{aligned}\] Meanwhile, we have to take the mean for both LHS and RHS. Note that \[\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E} \Big [ \langle \xi_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle \Big ] & = \mathbb{E} \Big [ \langle g_k - f'(x_k), x_k - x^\star \rangle\Big ]\\ & = \mathbb{E} \Big [ \mathbb{E} \big [ \langle g_k - f'(x_k) , x_k- x^\star \rangle \big ] | x_k\Big ]\\ & = \mathbb{E} \Big[ \langle \mathbb{E} [g_k | x_k] - \mathbb{E} [f'(x_k) |x_k ], x_k - x^\star \rangle \Big ]\\ & = \mathbb{E} \Big [ \langle f'(x_k) - f'(x_k) , x_k- x^\star \rangle \Big ] \\ & = 0 \end{aligned}\] Thus, we can say that \[\mathbb{E} \big [ f(\bar{x}_k) - f(x^\star) \big ] \leq \mathbb{E} \Big [ \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^K [f(x_k) - f(x^\star)] \Big ] \leq \frac{R^2}{2K\alpha_K} + \frac{1}{2K} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k M^2\] Remarks we can achieve the same convergence properties as in the non-noisy case although this subgradient method is slow, it can handle large amount of noise! Stepsize Analysis Choose $\alpha_k = \frac{R}{M\sqrt{k}}$ for each $k$. Then, \[\mathbb{E} [f(\bar{x}_k) ] - f(x^\star) \leq\frac{3RM}{2\sqrt{K}}\] Suppoe that $\sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k = \infty$ and $\alpha_k \rightarrow 0$ as $k\rightarrow \infty$. Then, for all $\epsilon>0$, \[\lim\sup_{k\rightarrow \infty} \mathbb{P} (f(\bar{x}_k ) -f(x^\star) \geq \epsilon) =0\] Finite sample guarantee Let \(\{\alpha_k \}\) be a non-increasing sequence of stepsizes, and assume that \(\|g\|_2\leq M\) for all stochastic subgradients $g$. Then, for any $\epsilon>0$, \[f(\bar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) \leq \frac{R^2}{2K\alpha_K} + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k}{2} M^2 + \frac{RM}{\sqrt{K}}\epsilon\] with probability at least $1-\exp (-\frac{1}{2}\epsilon^2)$. References S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/ Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

entry.title: [Optimization] Subgradient Methods
entry.content:
Motivation

How should we handle nondifferentiable objective functions?

  • objective functions can be convex but not differentiable
  • can’t use the standard optimality conditions
  • can’t use gradient or Hessian information in optimization algorithms

Subgradients

Definition

The subgradient set or subdifferential of a function $f$ at a point $x$ is defined as

\[\partial f(x) := \{ g \ \vert \ f(y) \geq f(x) + \langle g, y-x \rangle, \ \ \forall y\}\]

Intuitively, since a function is convex if and only if its epigraph $\text{epi } f$ is convex, the subgradient set $\partial f$ should be non-empty and consist of supporting hyperplanes of $\text{epi }f$.

If $f$ is conevx and diffeerentiable at $x$, then $\partial f(x) = { \nabla f(x)}$.

Optimality Condition

Theorem Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be convex. The point $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ minimizes $f$ over a convex set $C$ if and only if there exists a subgradient $g\in \partial f(x)$ such that simultaneously for all $y\in C$,

\[\langle g, y-x \rangle \geq 0\]

Intuition There is a vector $g$ in the subgradient set $\partial f(x)$ such that $-g$ is a supporting hyperplane to the feasible set $C$ at the point $x$. The direction of decrease of the function $f$ lie outside the set $C$.

If $C =\mathbb{R}^n$, $g=0$ is the only case where above inequality holds.

Calculus rules with subgradients

  • Scaling : if $h(x) = \alpha f(x)$ for some $\alpha \geq 0$, then

    \[\partial h(x) = \alpha \partial f(x)\]

  • Finite sum : Suppose $f_1, \cdots, f_m$ are convex functions and let $f:=\sum_{i=1}^m f_i$. Then,

    \[\partial f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m \partial f_i (x)\]

    where the addition is Minkowski sum.

    Proof Let $g_i \in \partial f_i(x)$ for all $i$. Then,

    \[\begin{aligned} f(y) = \sum_{i=1}^m f_i (y) & \geq \sum_{i=1}^m (f_i(x)+ \langle g_i , y-x\rangle )\\ & =\sum_{i=1}^m f_i (x) + \bigg \langle \sum_{i=1}^m g_i , y-x \bigg \rangle \end{aligned}\]

    we can see that

    \[\sum_i g_i \in \partial f(x)\]

  • Affine transformations : Let $f: \mathbb{R}^m \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be convex and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ and $b\in \mathbb{R}^m$. Then, $h: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $h(x) = f(Ax+b)$ is convex and has subdifferential

    \[\partial h(x) = A^T \partial f(Ax+b)\]

    Proof Let $g \in \partial f(Ax+b)$. Then,

    \[\begin{aligned} h(y) = f(Ay+b) & \geq f(Ax+b) + \langle g, (Ay+b) - (Ax+b) \rangle\\ & = h(x) + \langle g, A(y-x) \rangle\\ & = h(x) + \langle A^T g, y-x \rangle\\ \end{aligned}\]

    which shows that

    \[A^T g = A^T \partial f(Ax+b)\in \partial h(x)\]

Integrals with subgradients

Suppose that $f_s$ is convex for each $s\in S$, and let

\[f(x):= \int_S f_s (x) d\mu (s).\]

where $\mu$ is a positive measure on $S$. Then, if we let $g_s(x) \in \partial f_s(x)$ for each $s\in S$, we have

\[\int_S g_s (x) d\mu(s) \in \partial f(x)\]

Proof

\[\begin{aligned} \bigg \langle \int_S g_s (x) d\mu(s), y-x \bigg \rangle & = \int \langle g_s(x), y-x \rangle d\mu(s)\\ & \leq \int \Big ( f_s (y) - f_s(x) \Big )d\mu(s)\\ & = f(y)-f(x) \end{aligned}\]

Application to stochastic optimization

If we have a collection of functions $F:\mathbb{R}^n \times \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, where for each $s\in S$ the function $F(\cdot; s)$ is convex, then

\[f(x) := \mathbb{E}_s [ F(x; \mathcal{S}) ]\]

is convex when taking expectations over $\mathcal{S}$, and letting

\[g(x; s) \in \partial F(x;s)\]

gives a stochastic subgradient with the property that

\[\mathbb{E}_s [g(x;S)] \in \partial f(x).\]

Finite Maxima

Let $f_i$, $i=1,\cdots, m$ be convex functions and $f(x) := \max_i f_i (x)$. Then,

\[\text{epi }f = \bigcap_i \text{epi }f\]

Then, the subgradient set of $f$ is the convex hull of the subgradients of **active functions ** at $x$ (those attaining the maximum:

\[\partial f(x) = \text{Conv} \{ \partial f_i (x) : f_i(x) = f(x)\}\]

Proof For $y\in \mathbb{R}^n$, let $i$ be any index that $f_i (x) = f(x)$ (active index) and let $g_i \in \partial f_i (x)$.

\[\begin{aligned} f(y) \geq f_i (y) & \geq f_i (x) + \langle g_i, y-x \rangle\\ & = f(x) + \langle g_i, y-x \rangle \end{aligned}\]

Subgradient Methods

Motivation

Subgradient-based methods are essentially universally applicable for convex optimization problems, becuase they rely very little on the structure of the problem being solved.

  • This leads to effective but slow algorithms in classical optimization problems.
  • In large scale problems arising out of ML and statistical tasks, however, subgradient methods enjoy a number of (thoretical) optimality properties and have excellent practical performance.

Subgradient Algorithms

Recall that in gradient descent method, we have

\[x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(x_k)\]

where $\alpha_ k>0$ is a positive sequence of stepsizes.

Then, the subgradient algorithm iterates for $k=1,2,\cdots$, the following step:

  1. choose any subgradient

    \[g_k \in \partial f(x_k)\]

  2. Take the subgradient step

    \[x_{k+1}:= x_k - \alpha_k g_k.\]

Note that, subgradient descent does not guarantee $f(x_{k+1}) \not \leq f(x_k)$.

In this case, however, subgradients at least descend on a related quantity: the distance of $x$ to any optimal point.

Proof Let $g\in \partial f(x)$ and let $x^\star \in \arg\min f(x)$. Then, for any $\alpha$,

\[\frac{1}{2} \|x - \alpha g-x^\star\|_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}\|x - x^\star\|_2 ^2 -\alpha \langle g, x-x^\star\rangle + \frac{\alpha^2}{2} \|g \|_2^2\]

Then, by the definition of subgradients,

\[-\langle g, x-x^\star \rangle = \langle g, x^\star - x\rangle \leq f(x^\star) - f(x)\]

Therefore,

\[\frac{1}{2}\|x- \alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 -\alpha (f(x) - f(x^\star) ) + \frac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g\|_2 ^2.\]

We then conclude that, if $0<\alpha< \frac{2(f(x) - f(x^\star))}{|g|_2^2}$,

\[\|x- \alpha g- x^\star \|_2 ^2 \leq \|x - x^\star \|_2 ^2\]

If $0\not\in \partial f(x)$, then for any $x^\star\in \arg\min_x f(x)$ and any $g\in \partial f(x)$, there exists a stepsize $\alpha>0$ such that \(\|x-\alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 < \|x-x^\star\|_2^2\).

Convergence Gaurantees

We begin by stating a general result on the convergence of subgradient methods. We make a few simplifying assumptions in stating our result, several of which are not completely necessary, but which considerably simplify the analysis.

Assumption

  1. There is at least one (possibly non-unique) minimizing point \(x^\star \in \arg\min_x f(x)\) with \(f(x^\star) = \inf_x f(x) >-\infty\).
  2. The subgradients are bounded: for all $x$ and all $g\in \partial f(x)$, we have the subgradient \(\|g\|_2 \leq M <\infty\) (independently of $x$).

Theorem Let $\alpha_k$ be any non-negative sequence of stepsizes and the preceding assumptions hold. Let $x_k$ be generated by the subgradient iteration. Then for all $K\geq 1$,

\[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\]

Proof By using equation (20),

\[\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 & \leq \frac{1}{2}\|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star\|_2^2\\ & \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 - \alpha_k (f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ) + \frac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g_k\|_2^2 \end{aligned}\]

Rearranging this inequality, and using the bounded assumption \(\|g_k\|_2^2 \leq M^2\), we obtain

\[\alpha_k (f(x_k) -f(x^\star))\leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_k - x^\star\|_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 + \frac{\alpha_k^2}{2}M^2\]

By summing above equation from $k=1$ to $k=K$, we obtain

\[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\]

Weighted Averaging

Corollary Let $A_k:= \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i$ and define

\[\bar{x}_k = \frac{1}{A_K} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k x_k.\]

Then, we have

\[f(\bar{x}_k) -f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 -x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\]

Proof Note that $A_k^{-1} \sum_i \alpha_i =1$, and convexity of $f$ implies

\[\begin{aligned} f(\bar{x}_k) - f(x^\star) &\leq \frac{1}{\sum_i \alpha_i} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k f(x_k) - f(x^\star)\\ & = A_k^{-1} \bigg [ \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k (f(x_k)- f(x^\star)) \bigg ] \\ & =\frac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2A_k} \end{aligned}\]

Remarks

  • Suppose that

    \[\alpha_k \rightarrow 0 \ \ \ \ \text{and} \ \ \ \ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k = \infty\]

    Then,

    \[\frac{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2}{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k} \rightarrow 0\]

    and thus

    \[f(\bar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) \rightarrow 0 \ \ \text{as} \ \ K\rightarrow \infty\]

  • Since the subgradient method is not a descent method, we may just use the best point observed so far instead of the tracked weighted average, or the final point.

    \[x_k^{best} = \arg \min_{x_i, i\leq k} f(x_i)\]

    Then, we can say that

    \[f(x_k^{best}) - f(x^\star) \leq f(\bar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\]

Example

If we aim to minimize

\[f(x):= \frac{1}{m} \|Ax - b\|_1 = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m |\langle a_i, x \rangle-b_i |\]

we can design the subgradient as

\[g(x) = \frac{1}{m} A^T \text{sign}(Ax-b) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m a_i \text{sign} (\langle a_i, x \rangle-b_i )\in \partial f(x)\]

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

Subgradient Algorithms Recall that in gradient descent method, we have \[x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k 
abla f(x_k)\] where $\alpha_ k>0$ is a positive sequence of stepsizes. Then, the subgradient algorithm iterates for $k=1,2,\cdots$, the following step: choose any subgradient \[g_k \in \partial f(x_k)\] Take the subgradient step \[x_{k+1}:= x_k - \alpha_k g_k.\] Note that, subgradient descent does not guarantee $f(x_{k+1}) 
ot \leq f(x_k)$. In this case, however, subgradients at least descend on a related quantity: the distance of $x$ to any optimal point. Proof Let $g\in \partial f(x)$ and let $x^\star \in \arg\min f(x)$. Then, for any $\alpha$, \[ rac{1}{2} \|x - \alpha g-x^\star\|_2^2 =  rac{1}{2}\|x - x^\star\|_2 ^2 -\alpha \langle g, x-x^\starangle +  rac{\alpha^2}{2} \|g \|_2^2\] Then, by the definition of subgradients, \[-\langle g, x-x^\star angle = \langle g, x^\star - xangle \leq f(x^\star) - f(x)\] Therefore, \[ rac{1}{2}\|x- \alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 -\alpha (f(x) - f(x^\star) ) +  rac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g\|_2 ^2.\] We then conclude that, if $0<\alpha<  rac{2(f(x) - f(x^\star))}{|g|_2^2}$, \[\|x- \alpha g- x^\star \|_2 ^2 \leq \|x - x^\star \|_2 ^2\] If $0
ot\in \partial f(x)$, then for any $x^\star\in \arg\min_x f(x)$ and any $g\in \partial f(x)$, there exists a stepsize $\alpha>0$ such that \(\|x-\alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 < \|x-x^\star\|_2^2\). Convergence Gaurantees We begin by stating a general result on the convergence of subgradient methods. We make a few simplifying assumptions in stating our result, several of which are not completely necessary, but which considerably simplify the analysis. Assumption There is at least one (possibly non-unique) minimizing point \(x^\star \in \arg\min_x f(x)\) with \(f(x^\star) = \inf_x f(x) >-\infty\). The subgradients are bounded: for all $x$ and all $g\in \partial f(x)$, we have the subgradient \(\|g\|_2 \leq M <\infty\) (independently of $x$). Theorem Let $\alpha_k$ be any non-negative sequence of stepsizes and the preceding assumptions hold. Let $x_k$ be generated by the subgradient iteration. Then for all $K\geq 1$, \[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 +  rac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\] Proof By using equation (20), \[ egin{aligned}  rac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 & \leq  rac{1}{2}\|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star\|_2^2\ & \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 - \alpha_k (f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ) +  rac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g_k\|_2^2 \end{aligned}\] Rearranging this inequality, and using the bounded assumption \(\|g_k\|_2^2 \leq M^2\), we obtain \[\alpha_k (f(x_k) -f(x^\star))\leq  rac{1}{2} \|x_k - x^\star\|_2^2 -  rac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 +  rac{\alpha_k^2}{2}M^2\] By summing above equation from $k=1$ to $k=K$, we obtain \[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 +  rac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\] Weighted Averaging Corollary Let $A_k:= \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i$ and define \[ ar{x}_k =  rac{1}{A_K} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k x_k.\] Then, we have \[f( ar{x}_k) -f(x^\star) \leq  rac{\|x_1 -x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\] Proof Note that $A_k^{-1} \sum_i \alpha_i =1$, and convexity of $f$ implies \[ egin{aligned} f( ar{x}_k) - f(x^\star) &\leq  rac{1}{\sum_i \alpha_i} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k f(x_k) - f(x^\star)\ & = A_k^{-1}  igg [ \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k (f(x_k)- f(x^\star))  igg ] \ & = rac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2A_k} \end{aligned}\] Remarks Suppose that \[\alpha_k ightarrow 0 \ \ \ \ 	ext{and} \ \ \ \ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k = \infty\] Then, \[ rac{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2}{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k} ightarrow 0\] and thus \[f( ar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) ightarrow 0 \ \ 	ext{as} \ \ Kightarrow \infty\] Since the subgradient method is not a descent method, we may just use the best point observed so far instead of the tracked weighted average, or the final point. \[x_k^{best} = \arg \min_{x_i, i\leq k} f(x_i)\] Then, we can say that \[f(x_k^{best}) - f(x^\star) \leq f( ar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) \leq  rac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\] Example If we aim to minimize \[f(x):=  rac{1}{m} \|Ax - b\|_1 =  rac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m |\langle a_i, x angle-b_i |\] we can design the subgradient as \[g(x) =  rac{1}{m} A^T 	ext{sign}(Ax-b) =  rac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m a_i 	ext{sign} (\langle a_i, x angle-b_i )\in \partial f(x)\] References S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/ Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

entry.title: [Optimization] Projected Subgradient Methods
entry.content:
Recap

Subgradient Method

Recall that the subgradient algorithm which can be used to minimize an unconstrained convex function by the subgradient step

\[x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k g_k.\]

where $g_k\in \partial f(x)$. Even though that this subgradient step is not necessarily a descent method, but we proved that it decreases the distance of $x$ to any optimal point $x^\star$.

\[\|x-\alpha g- x^\star\|_2^2 < \|x- x^\star\|_2^2\]

We also proved that, the difference between our best estimate of the minimized objective \(f(x_K^{best})\) and the actual optimal point \(f(x^\star)\) is bounded above

\[f(x_K^{best})- f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 -x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\]

if the following assumptions holds

  1. subgradients are bounded : for all $x$ and all $g\in \partial f(x)$, we have the subgradient \(\|g\|_2\leq M<\infty\)
  2. there is at least one minimizing point $x^\star \in \arg \min_x f(x)$ with $f(x^\star) >-\infty$.

We can immediately see that our stepsize choise will very much effect the quality of subgradient method

  • If we use a constant step size (\(\alpha_k \equiv \alpha\)):

    \[f(x_K^{best}) - f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + K\alpha^2 M^2}{2K\alpha}\]

    we might end up having an upperbound that converges to nonzero value as $K\rightarrow \infty$, converging to $\alpha M^2/2$.

  • But fortunately, there’s a stepsize which can make the upperbound to converge to zero! It’s our good friend, square summable, but not summable stepsizes.

    \[\sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k^2 <\infty, \text{ and } \ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k =\infty\]

    In this case, we can see that the upper bound of equation (3) converges to zero. Thus,

    \[f(x_K^{best}) \rightarrow f(x^\star) \ \text{as} \ K\rightarrow \infty\]

Optimal Stepsize (when $f(x^\star)$ is known)

Let’s first assume that the optimal function value is known, but its optimal point is yet unknown.

Recall the useful inequality

\[\|x_{k+1}- x^\star \|_2^2 \leq \|x_k - x^\star\|_2^2 - 2\alpha_k (f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ) + \alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2\]

Thus, if we want to estimate $x^\star$ by iterating $x_k$, we might want to minimize the RHS of above inequality, which is btw a quadratic function with respect to $\alpha_k$. Thus, we might choose its minimizer as a stepsize

\[\alpha_k = \frac{f(x_k) - f(x^\star)}{\|g_k\|_2^2}\]

which is also known as the Polyak stepsize. We can see that Polyak stepsize effectively minimizes $f_{best}^{(k)} - f^\star$ then other stepsize choices.

When $f(x^\star)$ is unknown

We are going to approximate $f(x^\star)$ as

\[f(x^\star) \approx f(x_k^{best}) - \gamma_k\]

with $\sum_{k=1}^\infty \gamma_k <\infty, \sum_{k=1}^\infty \gamma_k = \infty $ and then apply the Polyak stepsize

\[\alpha_k =\frac{f(x_k ) - f(x_k^{best})+\gamma_k}{\|g_k \|_2^2 }\]

We can see that this is also a great stepsize ($\gamma_k = 10/(10+k)$)

Projected Subgradient Methods

Motivation

What if our objective function is nondifferentiable, and also constrained?

\[\begin{aligned} \min& \ \ f(x) \\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ x\in C \end{aligned}\]

where $f$ is a nondifferentiable convex set, and $C$ is the closed convex set.

Projection onto convex set

  • projection of $x$ onto $C$:

    \[\pi_C(x):= \arg \min_{y\in C} \|x-y\|_2\]

Note that this is a non-expansive operator

\[\|\pi_c(x) - \pi_c(y) \|_2 \leq \|x-y\|_2\]

Projected subgradient algorithm

Idea is very simple: it’s a subgradient algorithm with a slight additional process of projection.

\[x_{k+1} = \pi_c (x_k - \alpha_k g_k)\]

This is in fact equivalent to the process of

\[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} & = \arg \min_{y\in C} \|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - y\|_2^2\\ & = \arg \min _{y\in C} \bigg \{\|x_k - y\|_2^2 +\alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2 - 2\alpha_k \langle g_k , x_k - y \rangle \bigg \}\\ & = \arg \min_{x\in C}\bigg \{\|x- x_k\|_2^2 + 2\alpha_k \langle g_k, x- x_k \rangle + \alpha_k^2 \|g_k\|_2^2 \bigg\}\\ & = \arg \min_{x\in C} \bigg \{f(x_k) + \langle g_k , x- x_k\rangle + \frac{1}{2\alpha_k} \|x-x_k \|_2^2 \bigg \} \end{aligned}\]

Projected subgradient algorithm involves another optimization problem in each step. Thus, to make fast iterations, we may need a closed form solution of projection, or at least a fast way to calculate the projection.

Details / Example

Illustration

If we consider a problem

\[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ \frac{1}{2} \|Ax-b\|_2^2\\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \|x\|_1 \leq 1 \end{aligned}\]

some example iterations:

Projection on Affine set

If our feasible set is \(C= \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : Ax=b\}\) for \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}, m\leq n\), the projection onto $C$ can be calculated as

\[\begin{aligned} \min_z &\ \ \frac{1}{2} \|x- z\|_2^2 \\ \text{s.t.}&\ \ Az = b \end{aligned}\]

where its optimal solution $z^\star$ should satisfy the KKT optimality condition

\[Az^\star =b, \ \ x- z^\star +A^T \lambda =0\]

Then, we have

\[\lambda = (AA^T)^{-1}(b- Ax)\]

which leads to

\[\begin{aligned} \pi_c(x) = z^\star & = x+A^T (AA^T)^{-1}(b-Ax)\\ & = (I-A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A)x + A^T(AA^T)^{-1}b \end{aligned}\]

Thus our projected subgradient step becomes

\[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} = \pi_c(x_k - \alpha_k g_k) &= (I-A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A)x_k -\alpha_k (I-A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A)g_k +A^T(AA^T)^{-1}b\\ & = x_k - \alpha_k (I-A^T (AA^T)^{-1}A)g_k \end{aligned}\]

Projection on Norm balls

For feasible set \(C= \{x : \|x\|_p \leq 1\}\),

  • $p=\infty$ : clips the coordinates of $x$ to be in the range of [-1,1]
  • $p=2$: rescale (normalize)

Example : Least L1 Norm Problem

Consider the problem

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ f(x) = \|x\|_1\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax=b \end{aligned}\]

where the subgradient of $f$ is $g= \text{sign}(x)$. Projected subgradient update:

\[x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k (I- A^T (AA^T)^{-1}A)\text{sign}(x_k)\]

Setting a step size $\alpha_k = 0.1/k$.

Convergence Analysis

Assumptions

  1. Set $C\subset \mathbb{R}^n $ is compact and convex, and \(\|x- x^\star\|_2 \leq R <\infty\) for all $x\in C$.
  2. subgradients are bounded : \(\|g\|_2 \leq M <\infty\).

Theorem

Let $x_k$ be generated by the projected subgradient iteration, where the stepsizes $\alpha_k >0$ are non-increasing. Then,

\[\sum_{k=1}^K [f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ] \leq \frac{R^2}{2\alpha_K} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k M^2\]

Proof Recall that the projection operator is non-expnasive.

\[\begin{aligned} \|x_{k+1} - x^\star \|_2^2 & = \|\pi_c (x_k - \alpha_k g_k ) - \pi_c(x^\star) \|_2^2\\ & < \|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star \|_2^2 \\ & = \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 -2\alpha_k \langle g_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle + \alpha_k \|g_k \|_2^2 \\ & \leq \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 + 2\alpha_k (f(x^\star) - f(x_k) ) + \alpha_k \|g_k \|_2^2 \end{aligned}\]

Thus,

\[\begin{aligned} f(x_k ) - f(x^\star) \leq \frac{1}{2\alpha_k}\Big [ \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 \Big ] + \frac{\alpha_k}{2} \|g_k \|_2^2 \end{aligned}\]

which leads to

\[\begin{aligned} \sum_{k=1}^K \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ]& \leq \sum_{k=1}^K \bigg [ \frac{1}{2\alpha_k} \Big ( \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \|x_{k+1} - x^\star \|_2 ^2 \Big ) + \frac{\alpha_k}{2} \|g_k \|_2^2 \bigg ] \\ & \leq \frac{1}{2\alpha_1} \|x_1 - x^\star \|_2^2 + \sum_{k=2}^K \bigg ( \frac{1}{2\alpha_k}- \frac{1}{2\alpha_{k-1}}\bigg ) \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 \\ & \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ - \frac{1}{2\alpha_{k+1}}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star \|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k}{2}\|g_k \|_2^2\\ & \leq \frac{R^2}{2\alpha_K} + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k M^2}{2} \end{aligned}\]

Possible stepsize $\alpha_k = \alpha / \sqrt{k}$

If we deifine average \(\bar{x}_K = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K x_k\) and \(\alpha_k = \frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{k}}\). Then,

\[\begin{aligned} f(\bar{x}_K ) -f(x^\star)& = f\bigg ( \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^K x_k \bigg ) - f(x^\star) \\ & \leq \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K \Big ( f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ) \\ & = \frac{R^2}{2K\alpha_K} + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k M^2}{2K}\\ & = \frac{R^2}{2\alpha \sqrt{K}}+ \frac{\alpha M^2}{2K}\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}\\ & = \frac{R^2}{2\alpha \sqrt{K}}+ \frac{\alpha M^2}{2K} \int_0^K \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}dt \\ & = \frac{R^2}{2\alpha \sqrt{K}}+ \frac{\alpha M^2}{\sqrt{K}} \end{aligned}\]

Thus, we can see that convergence is gauranteed at the best rate $1/\sqrt{K}$.

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

Even though that this subgradient step is not necessarily a descent method, but we proved that it decreases the distance of $x$ to any optimal point $x^\star$. \[\|x-\alpha g- x^\star\|_2^2 < \|x- x^\star\|_2^2\] We also proved that, the difference between our best estimate of the minimized objective \(f(x_K^{best})\) and the actual optimal point \(f(x^\star)\) is bounded above \[f(x_K^{best})- f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 -x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\] if the following assumptions holds subgradients are bounded : for all $x$ and all $g\in \partial f(x)$, we have the subgradient \(\|g\|_2\leq M<\infty\) there is at least one minimizing point $x^\star \in \arg \min_x f(x)$ with $f(x^\star) >-\infty$. We can immediately see that our stepsize choise will very much effect the quality of subgradient method If we use a constant step size (\(\alpha_k \equiv \alpha\)): \[f(x_K^{best}) - f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + K\alpha^2 M^2}{2K\alpha}\] we might end up having an upperbound that converges to nonzero value as $K\rightarrow \infty$, converging to $\alpha M^2/2$. But fortunately, there’s a stepsize which can make the upperbound to converge to zero! It’s our good friend, square summable, but not summable stepsizes. \[\sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k^2 <\infty, \text{ and } \ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k =\infty\] In this case, we can see that the upper bound of equation (3) converges to zero. Thus, \[f(x_K^{best}) \rightarrow f(x^\star) \ \text{as} \ K\rightarrow \infty\] Optimal Stepsize (when $f(x^\star)$ is known) Let’s first assume that the optimal function value is known, but its optimal point is yet unknown. Recall the useful inequality \[\|x_{k+1}- x^\star \|_2^2 \leq \|x_k - x^\star\|_2^2 - 2\alpha_k (f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ) + \alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2\] Thus, if we want to estimate $x^\star$ by iterating $x_k$, we might want to minimize the RHS of above inequality, which is btw a quadratic function with respect to $\alpha_k$. Thus, we might choose its minimizer as a stepsize \[\alpha_k = \frac{f(x_k) - f(x^\star)}{\|g_k\|_2^2}\] which is also known as the Polyak stepsize. We can see that Polyak stepsize effectively minimizes $f_{best}^{(k)} - f^\star$ then other stepsize choices. When $f(x^\star)$ is unknown We are going to approximate $f(x^\star)$ as \[f(x^\star) \approx f(x_k^{best}) - \gamma_k\] with $\sum_{k=1}^\infty \gamma_k <\infty, \sum_{k=1}^\infty \gamma_k = \infty $ and then apply the Polyak stepsize \[\alpha_k =\frac{f(x_k ) - f(x_k^{best})+\gamma_k}{\|g_k \|_2^2 }\] We can see that this is also a great stepsize ($\gamma_k = 10/(10+k)$) Projected Subgradient Methods Motivation What if our objective function is nondifferentiable, and also constrained? \[\begin{aligned} \min& \ \ f(x) \\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ x\in C \end{aligned}\] where $f$ is a nondifferentiable convex set, and $C$ is the closed convex set. Projection onto convex set projection of $x$ onto $C$: \[\pi_C(x):= \arg \min_{y\in C} \|x-y\|_2\] Note that this is a non-expansive operator \[\|\pi_c(x) - \pi_c(y) \|_2 \leq \|x-y\|_2\] Projected subgradient algorithm Idea is very simple: it’s a subgradient algorithm with a slight additional process of projection. \[x_{k+1} = \pi_c (x_k - \alpha_k g_k)\] This is in fact equivalent to the process of \[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} & = \arg \min_{y\in C} \|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - y\|_2^2\\ & = \arg \min _{y\in C} \bigg \{\|x_k - y\|_2^2 +\alpha_k^2 \|g_k \|_2^2 - 2\alpha_k \langle g_k , x_k - y \rangle \bigg \}\\ & = \arg \min_{x\in C}\bigg \{\|x- x_k\|_2^2 + 2\alpha_k \langle g_k, x- x_k \rangle + \alpha_k^2 \|g_k\|_2^2 \bigg\}\\ & = \arg \min_{x\in C} \bigg \{f(x_k) + \langle g_k , x- x_k\rangle + \frac{1}{2\alpha_k} \|x-x_k \|_2^2 \bigg \} \end{aligned}\] Projected subgradient algorithm involves another optimization problem in each step. Thus, to make fast iterations, we may need a closed form solution of projection, or at least a fast way to calculate the projection. Details / Example Illustration If we consider a problem \[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ \frac{1}{2} \|Ax-b\|_2^2\\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \|x\|_1 \leq 1 \end{aligned}\] some example iterations: Projection on Affine set If our feasible set is \(C= \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : Ax=b\}\) for \(A\in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}, m\leq n\), the projection onto $C$ can be calculated as \[\begin{aligned} \min_z &\ \ \frac{1}{2} \|x- z\|_2^2 \\ \text{s.t.}&\ \ Az = b \end{aligned}\] where its optimal solution $z^\star$ should satisfy the KKT optimality condition \[Az^\star =b, \ \ x- z^\star +A^T \lambda =0\] Then, we have \[\lambda = (AA^T)^{-1}(b- Ax)\] which leads to \[\begin{aligned} \pi_c(x) = z^\star & = x+A^T (AA^T)^{-1}(b-Ax)\\ & = (I-A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A)x + A^T(AA^T)^{-1}b \end{aligned}\] Thus our projected subgradient step becomes \[\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} = \pi_c(x_k - \alpha_k g_k) &= (I-A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A)x_k -\alpha_k (I-A^T(AA^T)^{-1}A)g_k +A^T(AA^T)^{-1}b\\ & = x_k - \alpha_k (I-A^T (AA^T)^{-1}A)g_k \end{aligned}\] Projection on Norm balls For feasible set \(C= \{x : \|x\|_p \leq 1\}\), $p=\infty$ : clips the coordinates of $x$ to be in the range of [-1,1] $p=2$: rescale (normalize) Example : Least L1 Norm Problem Consider the problem \[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ f(x) = \|x\|_1\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax=b \end{aligned}\] where the subgradient of $f$ is $g= \text{sign}(x)$. Convergence Analysis Assumptions Set $C\subset \mathbb{R}^n $ is compact and convex, and \(\|x- x^\star\|_2 \leq R <\infty\) for all $x\in C$. subgradients are bounded : \(\|g\|_2 \leq M <\infty\). Theorem Let $x_k$ be generated by the projected subgradient iteration, where the stepsizes $\alpha_k >0$ are non-increasing. Then, \[\sum_{k=1}^K [f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ] \leq \frac{R^2}{2\alpha_K} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k M^2\] Proof Recall that the projection operator is non-expnasive. \[\begin{aligned} \|x_{k+1} - x^\star \|_2^2 & = \|\pi_c (x_k - \alpha_k g_k ) - \pi_c(x^\star) \|_2^2\\ & < \|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star \|_2^2 \\ & = \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 -2\alpha_k \langle g_k, x_k - x^\star \rangle + \alpha_k \|g_k \|_2^2 \\ & \leq \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 + 2\alpha_k (f(x^\star) - f(x_k) ) + \alpha_k \|g_k \|_2^2 \end{aligned}\] Thus, \[\begin{aligned} f(x_k ) - f(x^\star) \leq \frac{1}{2\alpha_k}\Big [ \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 \Big ] + \frac{\alpha_k}{2} \|g_k \|_2^2 \end{aligned}\] which leads to \[\begin{aligned} \sum_{k=1}^K \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ]& \leq \sum_{k=1}^K \bigg [ \frac{1}{2\alpha_k} \Big ( \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 - \|x_{k+1} - x^\star \|_2 ^2 \Big ) + \frac{\alpha_k}{2} \|g_k \|_2^2 \bigg ] \\ & \leq \frac{1}{2\alpha_1} \|x_1 - x^\star \|_2^2 + \sum_{k=2}^K \bigg ( \frac{1}{2\alpha_k}- \frac{1}{2\alpha_{k-1}}\bigg ) \|x_k - x^\star \|_2^2 \\ & \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ - \frac{1}{2\alpha_{k+1}}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star \|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k}{2}\|g_k \|_2^2\\ & \leq \frac{R^2}{2\alpha_K} + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k M^2}{2} \end{aligned}\] Possible stepsize $\alpha_k = \alpha / \sqrt{k}$ If we deifine average \(\bar{x}_K = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K x_k\) and \(\alpha_k = \frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{k}}\). Then, \[\begin{aligned} f(\bar{x}_K ) -f(x^\star)& = f\bigg ( \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^K x_k \bigg ) - f(x^\star) \\ & \leq \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^K \Big ( f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ) \\ & = \frac{R^2}{2K\alpha_K} + \sum_{k=1}^K \frac{\alpha_k M^2}{2K}\\ & = \frac{R^2}{2\alpha \sqrt{K}}+ \frac{\alpha M^2}{2K}\sum_{k=1}^K \frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}\\ & = \frac{R^2}{2\alpha \sqrt{K}}+ \frac{\alpha M^2}{2K} \int_0^K \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}dt \\ & = \frac{R^2}{2\alpha \sqrt{K}}+ \frac{\alpha M^2}{\sqrt{K}} \end{aligned}\] Thus, we can see that convergence is gauranteed at the best rate $1/\sqrt{K}$. References S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/ Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

entry.title: [Optimization] Approximation and Fitting
entry.content:
Norm Approximation

Problem Definition

We are considering

\[\min_{\mathbf{x}} \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\]

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ with $m\geq n$, and $|\cdot |$ is a norm on $\mathbb{R}^m $. This can be considered as a convex optimization problem, since norm is a convex function, and $\mathbf{Ax-b}$ is an affine transformation.

Interpretation

We can interpret this problem in many ways:

  1. geometric : $\mathbf{Ax}^\star$ is a point in $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{A})$ closest to $\mathbf{b}$.

  2. estimation : For a linear measurement model

    \[\mathbf{y = Ax+v}\]

    where $\mathbf{y}$ is the observation, $\mathbf{x}$ is unknown, and $\mathbf{v}$ is the measurement error, given $\mathbf{y=b}$, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the best guess of $\mathbf{x}$.

  3. optimal design : $\mathbf{x}$ is the design variable, where $\mathbf{Ax}$ is the result. $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the design choice that best approximates the desired result $\mathbf{b}$.

Example

  • least-squares approximation $| \cdot |_2$ :
\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \ \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_2^2 = \mathbf{(Ax-b)}^T\mathbf{(Ax-b)}\\ & \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ = \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}-2\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{b} \end{aligned}\]

the solution satisfies the normal equation

\[\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{Ax} = \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}\]

If $\text{rank } \mathbf{A}=n$ , $\mathbf{x}^\star = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$

  • chebysev approximation $|\cdot |_\infty$ :
\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \ \bigg ( \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_\infty = \max_i\ \big | \mathbf{a}_i^T \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}_i \big | \bigg ) \end{aligned}\]

this can be converted to LP

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ \ t \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ |\mathbf{a}_i ^T \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}_i |\leq t, \ \ \ i = 1,\cdots, m\\ & \ \ \ \iff -t\mathbf{1} \leq \mathbf{Ax-b} \leq t\mathbf{1} \end{aligned}\]
  • sum of absolute residuals approximation $|\cdot |_1 $ :
\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \ \bigg ( \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_1 = \sum_i \ \big | \mathbf{a}_i^T \mathbf{x}-\mathbf{b}_i \big | \bigg ) \end{aligned}\]

this can be converted to LP

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ \ \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{y} \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \ |\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b} |\leq \mathbf{y}\\ & \ \ \ \iff -\mathbf{y} \leq \mathbf{Ax-b} \leq \mathbf{y} \end{aligned}\]

Penalty Function Approximation

Problem Defintion

In $l_p$-norm approximation, for $1\leq p< \infty$, the objective is

\[\big( |\mathbf{r}_1|^p+|\mathbf{r}_2|^p+ \cdots + |\mathbf{r}_m|^p\big ) ^{1/p}\]

where

\[\mathbf{r}_i = \mathbf{a}_i ^T \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}_i\]

In that case, if we define $\phi: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$,

\[\phi(r) = |r|^p\]

we can convert above problem into

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ \ \sum_{i=1}^m \phi (\mathbf{r}_i )\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \ \mathbf{r= Ax-b} \end{aligned}\]

We call $\phi$ a convex penalty function.

Examples

  • Quadratic : \(\phi(u) = u^2\)

  • Deadzone-linear with width $a$ : \(\phi(u) = \max \{0,\vert u\vert-a\}\)

  • Log-barrier with limit $a$ : \(\phi(u)= \begin{cases} -a^2 \log(1- (u/a)^2 ) & \ \text{if } |u| <a\\ \infty & \ \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\)

Huber penalty function

\[\phi_{\text{hub}}(u) = \begin{cases} u^2 & \ \text{if } |u| \leq M\\ M(2|u|-M)& \ \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]

This huber penalty function allows an approximation less sensitive to outliers. In case of a least-squares regression where we fit $(t,y)$ with a linear curve $y = \alpha + \beta t$, using a huber penalty function makes us to draw a line that’s way more robust to outliers.

\[\min \sum_i \phi_{\text{hub}}(y_i - \alpha- \beta t_i )\]

Least-Norm Problem

Problem Definition

Consider the problem

\[\begin{aligned} \min_{\mathbf{x}} & \ \ \|\mathbf{x}\|\\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \mathbf{Ax=b} \end{aligned}\]

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $m\leq n$ (rows of $\mathbf{A}$ are linearly independent) and $|\cdot |$ is a norm on $\mathbb{R}^m $. In case of $m=n$, $\mathbf{x}^\star = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Thus, we assume $m<n$.

Interpretation

  1. geometric : $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is a point in the affine set \(\{\mathbf{x} \vert \mathbf{Ax=b}\}\) with minimum distance to origin $\mathbf{0}$.
  2. estimation : $\mathbf{b=Ax}$ are perfect measurements of $\mathbf{x}$. Among those, $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the smallest estimate, consistent with the measurements.
  3. optimal design : $\mathbf{x}$ are design variables, $\mathbf{b}$ are required results: $\mathbf{x}^\star$ is the most efficient design that satisfies the requirements.

Examples

  • In case of $|\cdot |_2$, this problem can be solved by optimality conditions

    \[\begin{aligned} \min _\mathbf{x} & \ \ \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 ^2 = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x}\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \mathbf{Ax=b} \end{aligned}\]

    where the Lagrangian is defined by

    \[\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x, \nu}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} + \nu^T (\mathbf{Ax-b})\]

    Then, if we assume $(\mathbf{x}^\star, \nu^\star)$ as a primal and dual optimal point, below conditions must hold.

    \[2\mathbf{x}^\star+ \mathbf{A}^T \nu ^\star = \mathbf{0}, \ \ \mathbf{Ax}^\star =b\]

    From these, we have

    \[2\mathbf{Ax}^\star + \mathbf{AA}^T \mathbf{\nu}^\star = \mathbf{0}\\ \iff 2\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{AA}^T \mathbf{\nu}^\star =0 \\ \iff \nu^\star = -2(\mathbf{AA}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{b}\]

    and

    \[\mathbf{x}^\star = \mathbf{A}^T (\mathbf{AA}^T)^{-1}\mathbf{b}\]

    Note that $\text{rank } \mathbf{A} = m <n$ , and thus the matrix $\mathbf{AA}^T$ is invertible.

  • In case of $|\cdot |_1 $, this can be solved by LP

    \[\begin{aligned} \min _\mathbf{x} & \ \ \mathbf{1}^T \mathbf{y}\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ \mathbf{Ax=b}\\ & \ \ -\mathbf{y}\leq \mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{y} \end{aligned}\]

Regularized Approximation

Problem Definiion

Regularization is a common scalariziation method used to solve the bi-criterion problem. One form of regularization is to minimize the weighted sum of the objectives:

\[\min \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\| + \gamma \|\mathbf{x}\|\]

where $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ and norms on $\mathbb{R}^m$ and $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be different.

Tikhonov regularization

The most common form of regularization is the one with Euclidean norms, resulting in a conevx quadratic optimization problem.

\[\min \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|_2 ^2 + \delta \|\mathbf{x}\|_2 ^2 = \mathbf{x}^T (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}+ \delta \mathbf{I})\mathbf{x}- 2\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{Ax}+ \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}\]

This has an analytical solution of

\[\mathbf{x}^\star = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}+ \delta \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}\]

Optimal Input Design

Consider a linear dynamical system with input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$.

  1. tracking error with desired output $y_{des}$:

    \[J_{track} := \sum_{t=0}^N (y(t) - y_{des}(t))^2\]

  2. input variation :

    \[J_{der} := \sum_{t=0}^{N-1} (u(t+1)- u(t))^2\]

  3. input magnitude :

    \[J_{mag} := \sum_{t=0}^N u(t)^2\]

Regularized least-squares formulation:

\[\min_u J_{track} + \delta J_{der} + \eta J_{mag}\]

Signal Reconstruction

Problem Definition

Consider the multi-objective problem

\[\min_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}} \ (\|\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{x}_{cor} \|_2 , \phi(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) )\]

where $\mathbf{x}$ is unknown signal, and $\mathbf{x}_{cor} = \mathbf{x}+\mathbf{v}$ is observed corrupted version of $\mathbf{x}$, with additive noise $\mathbf{v}$. $\phi$ is regularization function or smoothing objective.

Example

  • Quadratic smoothing :

    \[\phi_{quad}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i )^2\]

  • Total variation smoothing :

    \[\phi_{tv}(\hat {\mathbf{x}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1}|\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{i+1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i |\]

Total variation vs Quadratic Smoothing

Total variation smoothing preserves sharp transitions in signal!

Robust Approximation

Problem Definition

We want to minimize \(\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\) with uncertain \(\mathbf{A}\). We can take two approaches:

  1. Stochastic : assume $\mathbf{A}$ is random, and minimize \(\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\).
  2. Worst-case : choose set of $\mathcal{A}$ of possible values of $\mathbf{A}$, and minimize the worst-case scenario, \(\sup_{\mathbf{A}\in \mathcal{A}} \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\).

Example

Let $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}_0 + u \mathbf{A}_1 $. Stochastic case minimizes

\[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{A}(u)\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|_2 ^2 , \ \ u \in \text{uni}[-1,1]\]

Worst-case minimizes

\[\sup_{u\in [-1,1]} \|\mathbf{A}(u) \mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}\|_2^2\]

The residual in three cases \(r(u) = \|\mathbf{A}(u)\mathbf{x}- \mathbf{b}\|_2\)

Stochastic Robust Least Squares

Let $\mathbf{A = \bar{A}+U}$, where $\mathbf{U}$ is random with $\mathbb{E}\mathbf{U}=\mathbf{0}$, and $\mathbb{E}\mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{U = P}$. Consider the problem

\[\min \ \mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{(\bar{A}+U)x-b}\|_2 ^2\]

This can be converted to the problem of

\[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{(\bar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{\bar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Px}\]

Hence, this problem is equivalent to LS problem

\[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{(\bar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{\bar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 +\|\mathbf{P}^{1/2}\mathbf{x}\|_2^2\]

for \(\mathbf{P} = \delta \mathbf{I}\), we obtain a Tikhonov regularized problem.

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

We can take two approaches: Stochastic : assume $\mathbf{A}$ is random, and minimize \(\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\). Worst-case : choose set of $\mathcal{A}$ of possible values of $\mathbf{A}$, and minimize the worst-case scenario, \(\sup_{\mathbf{A}\in \mathcal{A}} \|\mathbf{Ax-b}\|\). Consider the problem \[\min \ \mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{( ar{A}+U)x-b}\|_2 ^2\] This can be converted to the problem of \[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{( ar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{ ar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Px}\] Hence, this problem is equivalent to LS problem \[\mathbb{E} \|\mathbf{( ar{A} + U)x - b}\|_2 ^2 =\|\mathbf{ ar{A}x-b}\|_2 ^2 +\|\mathbf{P}^{1/2}\mathbf{x}\|_2^2\] for \(\mathbf{P} = \delta \mathbf{I}\), we obtain a Tikhonov regularized problem.
